title: $500k repair bill: The kitchen time-bomb that could void your insurance
full_text:
Queensland homeowners are being warned they could face massive repair bills and voided insurance policies as a surge in bursting flexible hoses wreaks havoc in suburban kitchens and bathrooms.
New data released by Suncorp insurance has found a burst flexi-hoses have cost Queenslanders on average $30,000, up from $25,000 last year, with 6 per cent of claims exceeding $100,000 in damages.
While flexi-hoses are not illegal, they can only be legally installed by professionals and will deteriorate over time.
In worst cases, they have caused up to $500,000 worth of damage, if leaks go undetected.
More than half of Queenslanders appear unaware of the hidden issue, with 60 per cent reporting they had never had their connections checked or inspected.
Paul Callanan, head of claims and assessing at Suncorp said on average, two flexi-hoses in every household were ready to burst. Flexi-hoses can last five to 30 years depending on the brand and manufacturing of the hose.
“Flexi-hoses aren’t designed to last forever. They degrade silently, and by the time you notice a problem, it’s often too late,” Mr Callanan said.
Mr Callanan said in one extreme case, a Queensland family suffered $500,000 of damage to their home after a flexi-hose burst in their bathroom while they were holidaying overseas.
The leak was contained after two days, but it was enough to destroy the interior of the home.
“The family was displaced for months while the home was gutted and rebuilt, with the final bill costing more than $500,000,” he said.
“Water tore through the master bedroom, ensuite, four additional bedrooms, the kitchen, lounge, and study.”
According to Suncorp, one in 10 home insurance claims made last year were caused by flexi-hoses.
“With building and labour costs escalating due to external factors outside our control, and an increasing trend to open-plan home design, we’re only going to see the costs of these flexi-hose failures increase,” Mr Callanan said.
flexible hoses poll
Plumbers association deputy CEO Ernie Kretschmer said those who install non-compliant, uncertified or DIY hoses could face stiff penalties.
Mr Kretschmer said the spike in water damage claims could be triggered by dodgy DIY renovations, manufacturer faults or Queenslanders not conducting regular checks.
“It’s illegal for unlicensed people to install flexi-hoses, and would void your home insurance as well,” he said.
Mr Kretschmer said poor installations or the use of corrosive chemicals can lead to damage of the hoses, and homeowners should be checking under the sink every 12 months to ensure hoses are in good condition.
“It is the homeowners’ responsibility to maintain their plumbing and drainage,” he said.
Brisbane plumber, Rebecca Senyard said flexi-hoses people could potentially save hundreds of thousands of dollars in repairs costs simply by carrying out regular checks.
“They will burst, its just a matter of when,” she said.
“Preventive maintenance is going to be so much cheaper than being caught unaware.
“There are different products available to flexi-hoses such as No Blows which are a malleable, burst-proof, lead-free copper.
“A lot more of the newer homes are more likely to have flexi-hose as they are cost effective and less labour involved.”

summary: Queensland homeowners are at risk of huge repair bills and insurance issues due to bursting flexible hoses, which can cause severe water damage. Many homeowners are unaware of the need for regular checks, with some damages reaching up to $500,000.
author: Tayla Couacaud
heading: General Insurance
key_points: Average repair cost for burst flexi-hoses is $30,000, up from $25,000 last year.
Flexi-hoses can last 5 to 30 years but degrade over time.
One family faced $500,000 in damages while on holiday.
1 in 10 home insurance claims last year were due to flexi-hoses.
Homeowners are responsible for regular checks and maintenance.
ticker_line: Queensland homeowners face flexi-hose repair risks
